category_title: Typical children who exhibit egocentric moral orientations focused on personal consequences
minimal_context: The student is participating in a one-on-one interview and is asked to recall a personal experience of anger.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Can you tell me about a time when you felt really angry about something that happened to you?

STUDENT: My other little brother, my stepbrother Ned, he... I had this action figure.

TUTOR: Go on.

STUDENT: He doesn’t really know what my dad’s hammer does, but he took my dad’s hammer out of his tool belt and slammed in on my action figure and he broke it into pieces that were like an inch tall.

TUTOR: What happened next?

STUDENT: (Visibly agitated) I was just so mad that my pressure just got out of rage and I yelled at him! Then they’ll try to do something that they weren’t supposed to do.

options.A: Develop a series of real-life social stories that explicitly describe the beneficial course of action.
options.B: Employ reflective journaling where the student shares their thoughts and emotions about the interview process.
options.C: Move beyond anger coping strategies and address resolving the underlying anger.
options.D: Use a self-evaluation report each term to assist the student in expanding their goal setting.
